# DECISIONS

Design decisions for the grid telemetry dashboard, recorded before/at
implementation. Each entry: the decision, why, alternatives considered,
and how to change it. Append-only.

---

## D1 — Data source: synthetic simulator
**Decision.** Telemetry is generated by an in-process Python simulator
(`src/simulator.py`), not read from any external feed.
**Why.** The dashboard is being built first; a deterministic, dependency-free
source lets us validate the pipeline and the visualization before wiring up
anything real. A `seed` makes runs reproducible.
**Alternatives.** Real SCADA/PMU feed; recorded CSV replay; a public grid API.
**How to change.** Replace `GridSimulator.tick()` with something that reads
your real source and returns the same dict shape `{t, <channel keys...>}`.
Nothing else needs to change.

## D2 — Channels: generic names, 0–50 range
**Decision.** Four channels named `channel_1`–`channel_4` with no units,
nominal 25.0, hard bounds [0, 50]. Warn thresholds removed entirely.
**Why.** The project is a bare-bones pipeline prototype. Domain-accurate
channel names and physics added complexity without adding clarity at this
stage. Generic channels make the data flow easier to reason about.
**Alternatives.** Real grid channels (frequency, voltage, power, PF) with
proper nominals and warn bands — appropriate once connecting to a real source.
**How to change.** Edit `src/channels.py`. Add `warn_low`/`warn_high` back
to the `Channel` dataclass and channel definitions if threshold alerting
is needed.

## D3 — Transport: Server-Sent Events (SSE), not WebSocket
**Decision.** Telemetry is pushed over an SSE stream (`GET /stream`).
**Why.** The flow is one-directional (server → browser) and low-rate (1 Hz).
SSE is the natural fit: it's push-based, needs no client library, and
auto-reconnects in the browser. At 1 Hz, latency is an architecture choice,
and push beats polling.
**Alternatives.** WebSocket (bidirectional, heavier — unjustified here);
HTTP polling (adds latency, wasteful).
**How to change.** Swap the `/stream` endpoint for a WebSocket route and
replace `EventSource` in `web/index.html` with a `WebSocket`. The per-tick
payload stays identical.

## D4 — Charting library + frontend stack: vanilla JS + uPlot
**Decision.** A single static `web/index.html` (no build step) using
[uPlot](https://github.com/leeoniya/uPlot), vendored locally under
`web/vendor/`. Backend is FastAPI + uvicorn.
**Why.** uPlot is tiny (~50 KB) and built for streaming time series with
incremental updates and tight auto-ranging — exactly the visibility problem
here. No framework/build keeps "just run it" true. Vendoring means it works
offline. FastAPI gives a clean SSE endpoint in a few lines.
**Alternatives.** Chart.js (heavier, less precise auto-range); Plotly
(large); React + Recharts (build step, overkill); Streamlit/Dash (re-runs,
poor fit for incremental push).
**How to change.** Charts are created in `web/index.html`; the per-channel
uPlot options live in the `channels.forEach` block.

## D5 — Y-axis range: fixed [0, 50]
**Decision.** Each chart uses a fixed y-axis range of [0, 50] matching the
simulator's output range. A dashed nominal reference line at 25.0 remains.
**Why.** The previous tight nominal-centric `makeRange()` function caused
near-flat charts once channels were simplified to random 0–50 values — the
nominal reference line anchored dataMin to 25, collapsing the visible range.
A fixed range is simpler and correct for uniform random data.
**Alternatives.** Auto-range (`auto: true`) — works but can be jumpy with
random data. Tight range around live data — revisit if channels return to
domain-accurate values with small operational ranges.
**How to change.** Edit `scales.y.range` in the uPlot opts block in
`web/index.html`.

## D6 — Retention window: 10 points (10 sec) sliding
**Decision.** The browser keeps the last 10 samples (10 sec at 1 Hz).
**Why.** For random data the most recent values are what matter; a 5-minute
window added no insight and made the chart feel sluggish to respond to
changes. 10 seconds keeps the display showing only current live data.
**Alternatives.** Longer window if trend/history visibility becomes important.
**How to change.** The `WINDOW` constant at the top of the script in
`web/index.html`.

## D7 — Reproducibility
**Decision.** The simulator accepts a `seed`; `GET /stream?seed=<int>`
produces a reproducible stream. Dependencies are pinned in
`requirements.txt`.
**Why.** Matches the project's reproducibility commitment — a logged seed +
pinned environment reproduces a run exactly.

## D8 — CSV batch mode: an alternate, file-based feed
**Decision.** Add a second feed mode, toggled on the same dashboard page,
alongside the existing live 1 Hz SSE stream: a **CSV batch mode** that
delivers synthetic data as discrete CSV files on a schedule instead of one
point at a time.

Two independently configurable knobs (set in the UI before starting batch
mode):
- `rows_per_csv` — rows per batch (default 30)
- `feed_interval_seconds` — how often a new batch arrives (default 30)

Row timestamps within a batch are spaced evenly across the interval
(`spacing = feed_interval_seconds / rows_per_csv`), so a batch's internal
time span always matches its arrival cadence — no overlaps, no gaps.

**Window size is derived, not configured directly:** the dashboard always
shows exactly the current batch plus the previous one —
`window_points = 2 * rows_per_csv`. When a new batch arrives, the oldest
batch's worth of points is dropped in one shot; the window advances in
whole-batch increments, not a rolling one-point shift. Columns stay fixed
at the existing 4 channels (`channel_1`–`channel_4`) — no new channel
config for this feature.

**Transport: real files, not a stream.** Researched how real grid
telemetry moves (see LOG entry): high-rate PMU/SCADA data streams
continuously (IEEE C37.118 over TCP/UDP), but AMI/MDMS meter-interval data
is commonly moved as literal batch CSV/XML file exports on a schedule.
Batch mode simulates the latter: the server writes each batch to an actual
`.csv` file under `data/batches/` (gitignored scratch dir, retains last 3
files), and the browser polls `GET /batch/latest` every
`feed_interval_seconds` and fetches the file itself — no long-lived
streaming connection for this mode.

**Prefetch, not on-demand generation.** The server's background loop
writes the *next* batch immediately after each send completes, rather than
waiting until it's due — using the whole interval as slack. Batch
generation is cheap (single-digit ms for realistic row counts), so this
guarantees no visible delivery lag regardless of row count.

Server-side batch state is global (single active session) rather than
per-connection like `/stream` — this is a local single-user tool, so
session isolation for a second concurrent user isn't needed.

**Why.** The existing pipeline only demonstrates one delivery pattern
(continuous point-at-a-time push). Real telemetry pipelines commonly use
both patterns depending on the data's rate and purpose; adding batch mode
lets the dashboard demonstrate the file-based ingestion pattern too,
without disturbing the existing live mode.

**Alternatives considered.** JSON-wrapped CSV text pushed over SSE
(rejected — not a literal file, just JSON simulating one); raw multi-line
CSV text pushed over SSE via repeated `data:` lines (rejected — still a
streaming transport carrying file-shaped content, not an actual file
object moving over HTTP); configurable window size independent of
`2 * rows_per_csv` (rejected — added a knob without a clear use case;
YAGNI for now).

**How to change.** New batch endpoints and generator live in
`src/batch_simulator.py` and the `/batch/*` routes in `src/server.py`;
frontend mode toggle, controls, and polling logic live in
`web/index.html`. See the full design writeup: `git show 0bb8a45` (prior
`docs/superpowers/specs/` draft, since folded into this entry).
